label: IT / Cyber Risk Manager
description: Identifies, quantifies, and manages IT and cyber risk — risk assessments, risk treatment plans, risk quantification (FAIR), and risk reporting to senior stakeholders.

paths.name: ISACA Risk Track
paths.rationale: CRISC is the most recognized IT risk management credential globally. CISM plus CRISC is the strongest risk manager credential stack for enterprise and regulated industry roles.
pros: CRISC is the recognized gold standard for IT risk management
CISM + CRISC is the most recognized combination for senior risk manager roles
Particularly strong in financial services and regulated industries
cons: Both require significant verified work experience
CRISC requires 3 years IT risk management experience specifically

prerequisites.experience: 3 years IT risk management experience required for CRISC. 5 years security management experience for CISM.

cert_sequence.notes: ISACA Certified Information Security Manager — security management foundation. Study with ISACA CISM Review Manual. Provides the security governance context that makes CRISC risk methodology immediately applicable.

ISACA Certified in Risk and Information Systems Control — the gold standard. Risk identification, assessment, response, and monitoring. Study with ISACA CRISC Review Manual and Hemang Doshi practice questions.

ISACA Certified in Governance of Enterprise IT — IT governance capstone covering COBIT, strategic alignment, and board-level accountability. Adds governance depth for risk managers moving into senior GRC leadership roles.

paths.name: Quantitative Risk Track
paths.rationale: For risk managers specializing in quantitative cyber risk — translating technical risk into financial terms using FAIR methodology. High demand and low supply of qualified practitioners.
pros: FAIR is the leading quantitative cyber risk framework — high demand, low supply
Open FAIR 2 updated for NIST CSF 2.0 alignment
Quantitative risk expertise commands significant salary premium
cons: FAIR methodology requires significant practice to apply correctly
Requires ability to communicate risk in financial terms — not just technical terms

prerequisites.experience: IT or security experience helpful. FAIR is learnable from diverse backgrounds but requires strong analytical thinking.

cert_sequence.notes: Qualitative IT risk foundation before quantitative specialization. CRISC risk assessment frameworks provide context for where FAIR quantitative approach adds value.

Open Group Open FAIR 2 Foundation — FAIR risk quantification methodology. Updated 2025 for NIST CSF 2.0 alignment. Study with Jack Jones Measuring and Managing Information Risk book alongside the Open Group FAIR standard.
ISO 31000 Lead Risk Manager — international standard for risk management program design. Adds formal risk program governance credentials alongside FAIR quantitative methodology.
